    true;

can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, Threshold) ->
    % 1. Build Adjacency List: Adj[u] = [[v1, cost1], [v2, cost2], ...]
    Adj = lists:foldl(build_adj_list, #{}, Edges),
    
    % 2. 0-1 BFS setup
    % Distances: map[Node] = min_heavy_edges
    InitialDist = maps:new(),
    maps:put(InitialDist, Source, 0),

    % Queue: List of [HeavyCount, Node]. We treat it as a deque for 0-1 BFS.
    % Front is for cost 0, Back is for cost 1.
    InitialQueue = [[0, Source]],

    % 3. BFS traversal
    do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, InitialDist, InitialQueue).

build_adj_list(Edge, Acc) when is_list(Edge) ->
    [U, V, W] = Edge,
    is_heavy = W > Threshold,
    cost = if is_heavy -> 1; true -> 0 end,
    
    % Add edge U->V and V->U
    NewAcc = maps:update(Acc, U, fun(List) -> [[V, cost]|List] end),
    maps:update(NewAcc, V, fun(List) -> [[U, cost]|List] end).

% Helper function for 0-1 BFS
do_bfs(_, Target, _K, _Adj, Dist, []) ->
    maps:get(Dist, Target, -1) == -1. % Target not reached
do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, Dist, [[D, U]|RestQueue]) when D <= K ->
    if U == Target ->
        true; % Target reached with <= K heavy edges
    end;
do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, Dist, [[D, U]|RestQueue]) when D > K ->
    % Current path is already invalid, skip it and continue
    do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, Dist, RestQueue);

do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, Dist, Queue) ->
    % Process current node U from the front of the queue
    [D, U | RestQueue] = Queue,
    
    % Get neighbors of U
    Neighbors = maps:get(Adj, U, []),
    
    % Process neighbors and generate new queue elements
    NewQueueItems = [
        {V, Cost} || [V, Cost] <- Neighbors
    ],
    
    % Update Distances and Queue
    {NewDist, NewQueue} = lists:foldl(process_neighbor, {Dist, RestQueue}, NewQueueItems),
    
    do_bfs(N, Target, K, Adj, NewDist, NewQueue).

process_neighbor({V, Cost}, {CurrentDist, CurrentQueue}) ->
    NewD = D + Cost,
    
    % Check if we found a better path to V
    OldD = maps:get(CurrentDist, V, K+1),
    
    if NewD < OldD ->
        % Update distance
        UpdatedDist = maps:put(CurrentDist, V, NewD),
        
        % Add to the front (cost 0) or back (cost 1) of the queue
        NewQueue = if Cost == 0 -> [NewD, V] ++ CurrentQueue; true -> CurrentQueue ++ [[NewD, V]] end,
        
        {UpdatedDist, NewQueue};
    
    {CurrentDist, CurrentQueue}.


solve(N, Edges, Source, Target, K) when Source == Target ->
    0;

solve(N, Edges, Source, Target, K) ->
    % Binary Search for the minimum Threshold T
    % Search range: L=0 to R=10^9 + 2 (exclusive upper bound)
    % Max weight is 10^9. Threshold T=10^9 means all edges are light. T=10^9+1 means nothing is defined, but R needs to cover all possibilities.
    
    MaxWeight = lists:foldl(find_max_weight, 0, Edges),
    
    L = 0,
    R = MaxWeight + 2, % Search space [0, MaxWeight + 1]
    
    find_min_threshold(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, L, R, -1).

find_min_threshold(_, _, _, _, _, L, R, Result) when L >= R ->
    Result;

find_min_threshold(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, L, R, CurrentMin) ->
    M = L + (R - L) div 2,
    
    if can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, M) ->

        NewMin = M,
        NewR = M,
        find_min_threshold(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, L, NewR, NewMin);
    else
        % M does not work. Need a higher threshold.
        NewL = M + 1,
        find_min_threshold(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, NewL, R, CurrentMin).

find_max_weight(Edge, MaxW) when is_list(Edge) ->
    [_, _, W] = Edge,
    MaxW = max(MaxW, W).

% --- Binary Search Helper ---
% do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) -> minThreshold
do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) when L >= R ->
    % If L exceeded the safe upper bound, no path exists even with all edges light.
    case can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, 1000000001) of
        true -> L-1; % Should not happen if L>=R, but handles edge case where BS ends exactly on the solution.
        false -> -1
    end;
do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) ->
    M = L + (R - L) div 2,
    case can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, M) of
        true ->
            % M works, try smaller threshold
            do_binary_search(L, M, N, Edges, Source, Target, K);
        false ->
            % M does not work, need higher threshold
            do_binary_search(M + 1, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K)
    end.

% --- Reachability Check (BFS/Constrained Shortest Path) ---
% can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, Threshold) -> boolean
can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, Threshold) ->
    % 1. Build Adjacency List
    Adj = build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold),

    % 2. BFS initialization
    % Queue stores: [(Node, HeavyCount), ...]
    Queue = [[Source, 0]],
    % Visited map stores: {Node, Count} -> true (to avoid redundant processing)
    Visited = #{ {Source, 0} => true },

    % 3. Start BFS traversal
    bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K)

% Helper to build adjacency list
build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold) ->
    % Initialize N empty lists
    Adj = lists:duplicate(N, []),
    lists:foldl(
        fun(acc, [U, V, W]) ->
            % Cost is 1 if heavy (W > Threshold), 0 if light (W <= Threshold)
            Cost = if W > Threshold -> 1; true -> 0 end,
            
            % Add edge U -> V
            Adj_UV = [[V, Cost] | lists:nth(U, acc)],
            % Add edge V -> U (undirected)
            Adj_VU = [[U, Cost] | lists:nth(V, acc)],
            
            % Update list at U and V
            lists:replace(acc, U, Adj_UV),
            lists:replace(lists:replace(acc, V, Adj_VU), V, Adj_VU) % Fix for list update in Erlang structure
        end,
        Adj,
        Edges
    ).
    % NOTE: Direct list manipulation for graph building is inefficient in pure Erlang.
    % In a real scenario, we would use dictionaries/maps for sparse adjacency lists. 
    % Since N is up to 1000, we stick to the list representation mimicking an array of lists for functional correctness,

% Redefining build_adj_list using lists/maps for better Erlang style, focusing on functional correctness
build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold) ->
    % Start with N empty lists
    Adj_map = lists:keymap(N, 0, []),

    % Fold over edges to build the adjacency list structure
    Adj_list = lists:foldl(
        fun(AccMap, [U, V, W]) ->
            Cost = if W > Threshold -> 1; true -> 0 end,
            
            % Helper to add neighbor to a specific node's list in the map
            add_neighbor(AccMap, Node, Neighbor, Cost) =
                lists:update(AccMap, Node, fun(Neighbors) -> [[Neighbor, Cost] | Neighbors] end),
            
            % Update for U -> V
            AccMap1 = add_neighbor(AccMap, U, V, Cost),
            
            % Update for V -> U
            AccMap2 = add_neighbor(AccMap1, V, U, Cost),
            
            AccMap2
        end,
        Adj_map,
        Edges
    ),
    Adj_list.

% Helper for safe list replacement/update in Erlang context (mimics array update)
lists:update(List, Index, Fun) ->
    case List of
        [H | T] when Index == 0 -> [Fun(H) | T];
        [H | T] -> 
            case lists:update(T, Index - 1, Fun) of
                NewT -> [H | NewT]
            end;
        _ -> []
    end.

% --- BFS implementation (Tail-Recursive) ---
% bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K) -> boolean
bfs([], _, _, _, _) ->
    false; % Queue empty, target not reached

bfs([[U, C] | Q], Visited, Adj, Target, K) ->
    if U == Target ->
        true; % Target reached successfully
    else
        % Process neighbors of U
        case lists:lookup(U, Adj) of
            {Ok, Neighbors} ->
                New_Q = [],
                New_Visited = Visited,
                
                % Fold over neighbors to generate new states and update queue/visited
                {New_Q, New_Visited} = lists:foldl(
                    fun(AccState, [V, Cost]) ->
                        New_C = C + Cost,
                        if New_C <= K ->
                            State = {V, New_C},
                            case maps:lookup(State, AccState#Visited) of
                                {ok, _} -> % Already visited this state
                                _ -> % Not visited
                                    % Add to queue and mark visited
                                    New_State = AccState#Visited,
                                    New_State.Queue = [[V, New_C] | AccState.Queue],
                                    New_State.Visited = maps:insert(State, true, AccState.Visited)
                            end
                        else
                            AccState % Too many heavy edges
                    end,
                    % Initial state for the fold: Queue is the rest of the old queue Q, Visited is the current map
                    % We need to pass the state explicitly for mutation simulation
                    #Visited{Queue = Q}, 
                    Neighbors
                ),
                
                % Continue BFS recursively
                bfs(New_Q, New_Visited, Adj, Target, K);

            {Error, _} -> 
                % Should not happen if graph structure is correct
                bfs(Q, Visited, Adj, Target, K)
        end
    end.

% Since standard Erlang lists don't allow easy functional queue updates while maintaining state, 
% and the iterative nature of BFS relies on appending/prepending, we must simulate the state transition carefully. 
% We will simplify the state passing to adhere to standard functional programming practices, even if performance might be slightly impacted by list manipulation overhead.

bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K) ->
    case Queue of
        [] -> false;
        [[U, C] | Q_tail] ->
            if U == Target ->
                true;
            else
                % Find neighbors of U
                case lists:lookup(U, Adj) of
                    {ok, Neighbors} ->
                        % Generate new states
                        New_States = [
                            {V, C + Cost}
                            || [V, Cost] <- Neighbors, C + Cost <= K
                        ],
                        
                        % Filter out already visited states
                        Unvisited_New_States = [
                            S || S <- New_States, maps:not_member(S, Visited)
                        ],
                        
                        % Update Visited map
                        New_Visited = lists:foldl(
                            fun(Map, S) -> maps:insert(S, true, Map) end, 
                            Visited, 
                            Unvisited_New_States
                        ),
                        
                        % Add to queue (maintaining FIFO order requires prepending/reversing, but we just prepend and accept potential minor complexity trade-offs for functional purity)
                        New_Queue = [S || S <- Unvisited_New_States],
                        
                        % Continue BFS: New items are added to the end of the logical queue.
                        % In this recursive implementation, we append the new items to the end of Q_tail.
                        bfs(Q_tail ++ New_Queue, New_Visited, Adj, Target, K);

                    {error, _} ->
                        % Error in lookup, just move to next item
                        bfs(Q_tail, Visited, Adj, Target, K)
                end
        _ -> false % Should not happen if Queue structure is maintained correctly
    end.

solve(N, Edges, Source, Target, K) ->
    case Source == Target of
        true -> 0;
        false ->
            % Binary Search setup
            % L = 0 (Min possible threshold)
            % R = 10^9 + 2 (Exclusive upper bound, max weight + 2)
            do_binary_search(0, 1000000002, N, Edges, Source, Target, K)
    end.

% --- Binary Search Helper ---
% do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) -> minThreshold
do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) when L >= R ->
    % If L exceeded the safe upper bound, no path exists even with all edges light.
    case can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, 1000000001) of
        true -> L-1; % Should not happen if L>=R, but handles edge case where BS ends exactly on the solution.
        false -> -1
    end;
do_binary_search(L, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K) ->
    M = L + (R - L) div 2,
    case can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, M) of
        true ->
            % M works, try smaller threshold
            do_binary_search(L, M, N, Edges, Source, Target, K);
        false ->
            % M does not work, need higher threshold
            do_binary_search(M + 1, R, N, Edges, Source, Target, K)
    end.

% --- Reachability Check (BFS/Constrained Shortest Path) ---
% can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, Threshold) -> boolean
can_reach(N, Edges, Source, Target, K, Threshold) ->
    % 1. Build Adjacency List
    Adj = build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold),

    % 2. BFS initialization
    % Queue stores: [(Node, HeavyCount), ...]
    Queue = [[Source, 0]],
    % Visited map stores: {Node, Count} -> true (to avoid redundant processing)
    Visited = #{ {Source, 0} => true },

    % 3. Start BFS traversal
    bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K)

% Helper to build adjacency list
build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold) ->
    % Initialize N empty lists
    Adj = lists:duplicate(N, []),
    lists:foldl(
        fun(acc, [U, V, W]) ->
            % Cost is 1 if heavy (W > Threshold), 0 if light (W <= Threshold)
            Cost = if W > Threshold -> 1; true -> 0 end,
            
            % Add edge U -> V
            Adj_UV = [[V, Cost] | lists:nth(U, acc)],
            % Add edge V -> U (undirected)
            Adj_VU = [[U, Cost] | lists:nth(V, acc)],
            
            % Update list at U and V
            lists:replace(acc, U, Adj_UV),
            lists:replace(lists:replace(acc, V, Adj_VU), V, Adj_VU) % Fix for list update in Erlang structure
        end,
        Adj,
        Edges
    ).
    % NOTE: Direct list manipulation for graph building is inefficient in pure Erlang.
    % In a real scenario, we would use dictionaries/maps for sparse adjacency lists. 
    % Since N is up to 1000, we stick to the list representation mimicking an array of lists for functional correctness, 
    % assuming the built-in list manipulation functions are used carefully.

% Redefining build_adj_list using lists/maps for better Erlang style, focusing on functional correctness
build_adj_list(N, Edges, Threshold) ->
    % Start with N empty lists
    Adj_map = lists:keymap(N, 0, []),

    % Fold over edges to build the adjacency list structure
    Adj_list = lists:foldl(
        fun(AccMap, [U, V, W]) ->
            Cost = if W > Threshold -> 1; true -> 0 end,
            
            % Helper to add neighbor to a specific node's list in the map
            add_neighbor(AccMap, Node, Neighbor, Cost) =
                lists:update(AccMap, Node, fun(Neighbors) -> [[Neighbor, Cost] | Neighbors] end),
            
            % Update for U -> V
            AccMap1 = add_neighbor(AccMap, U, V, Cost),
            
            % Update for V -> U
            AccMap2 = add_neighbor(AccMap1, V, U, Cost),
            
            AccMap2
        end,
        Adj_map,
        Edges
    ),
    Adj_list.

% Helper for safe list replacement/update in Erlang context (mimics array update)
lists:update(List, Index, Fun) ->
    case List of
        [H | T] when Index == 0 -> [Fun(H) | T];
        [H | T] -> 
            case lists:update(T, Index - 1, Fun) of
                NewT -> [H | NewT]
            end;
        _ -> []
    end.

% --- BFS implementation (Tail-Recursive) ---
% bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K) -> boolean
bfs([], _, _, _, _) ->
    false; % Queue empty, target not reached

bfs([[U, C] | Q], Visited, Adj, Target, K) ->
    if U == Target ->
        true; % Target reached successfully
    else
        % Process neighbors of U
        case lists:lookup(U, Adj) of
            {Ok, Neighbors} ->
                New_Q = [],
                New_Visited = Visited,
                
                % Fold over neighbors to generate new states and update queue/visited
                {New_Q, New_Visited} = lists:foldl(
                    fun(AccState, [V, Cost]) ->
                        New_C = C + Cost,
                        if New_C <= K ->
                            State = {V, New_C},
                            case maps:lookup(State, AccState#Visited) of
                                {ok, _} -> % Already visited this state
                                _ -> % Not visited
                                    % Add to queue and mark visited
                                    New_State = AccState#Visited,
                                    New_State.Queue = [[V, New_C] | AccState.Queue],
                                    New_State.Visited = maps:insert(State, true, AccState.Visited)
                            end
                        else
                            AccState % Too many heavy edges
                    end,
                    % Initial state for the fold: Queue is the rest of the old queue Q, Visited is the current map
                    % We need to pass the state explicitly for mutation simulation
                    #Visited{Queue = Q}, 
                    Neighbors
                ),
                
                % Continue BFS recursively
                bfs(New_Q, New_Visited, Adj, Target, K);

            {Error, _} -> 
                % Should not happen if graph structure is correct
                bfs(Q, Visited, Adj, Target, K)
        end
    end.

% Since standard Erlang lists don't allow easy functional queue updates while maintaining state, 
% and the iterative nature of BFS relies on appending/prepending, we must simulate the state transition carefully. 
% We will simplify the state passing to adhere to standard functional programming practices, even if performance might be slightly impacted by list manipulation overhead.

bfs(Queue, Visited, Adj, Target, K) ->
    case Queue of
        [] -> false;
        [[U, C] | Q_tail] ->
            if U == Target ->
                true;
            else
                % Find neighbors of U
                case lists:lookup(U, Adj) of
                    {ok, Neighbors} ->
                        % Generate new states
                        New_States = [
                            {V, C + Cost}
                            || [V, Cost] <- Neighbors, C + Cost <= K
                        ],
                        
                        % Filter out already visited states
                        Unvisited_New_States = [
                            S || S <- New_States, maps:not_member(S, Visited)
                        ],
                        
                        % Update Visited map
                        New_Visited = lists:foldl(
                            fun(Map, S) -> maps:insert(S, true, Map) end, 
                            Visited, 
                            Unvisited_New_States
                        ),
                        
                        % Add to queue (maintaining FIFO order requires prepending/reversing, but we just prepend and accept potential minor complexity trade-offs for functional purity)
                        New_Queue = [S || S <- Unvisited_New_States],
                        
                        % Continue BFS: New items are added to the end of the logical queue.
                        % In this recursive implementation, we append the new items to the end of Q_tail.
                        bfs(Q_tail ++ New_Queue, New_Visited, Adj, Target, K);

                    {error, _} ->
                        % Error in lookup, just move to next item
                        bfs(Q_tail, Visited, Adj, Target, K)
                end
        _ -> false % Should not happen if Queue structure is maintained correctly
    end.
